name: The Waiting Room
description: Rows of plastic chairs, flickering overhead lights, a cracked clock with no hands. The number being called is always one before yours.

characters.name: The Attendant
characters.backstory: Calls numbers that never match yours. Glances at you without seeing.

encounters.question: Do you call the numbers randomly, or is there a pattern I'll never guess?

responses.text: Pattern's there, etched in the bureaucracy of waiting. You're just not on the list yet and maybe you were, and I forgot to announce it. Check your ticket; it's probably expired.
Random's too much effort. I call what the clipboard tells me, and it doesn't care about your guesswork. Keep waiting; that's the only rule that sticks.

encounters.question: Have you ever called your own number, or are you stuck here too?

responses.text: Once, maybe and I dreamed I did. Heard my name in the static, but the chair wouldn't let me stand. We're all waiting for something, even the ones behind the counter.
My number's not in the system. I checked. I'm just the voice, not the queue. Don't ask questions that make the lights flicker; it confuses the paperwork.

encounters.question: If I leave my seat, will my turn come up, or am I forfeit forever?

responses.text: Leave, and the system might notice and might skip you out of spite, might call you the second your shadow's gone. Forfeit’s a strong word, but the chairs remember who sat where.
Seats don't own your turn. Walk away, come back, it’s all the same. The numbers don’t care about your posture or your patience. They just keep rolling.

characters.name: Familiar Echo
characters.backstory: Keeps looking at you as if you should remember them.

encounters.question: Do I know you from somewhere, or is this place just playing tricks?

responses.text: You do, you did, you will and time’s a bad receptionist here, misfiling faces. I’m the one you forgot to call back, waiting since the clock lost its hands.
Tricks, always tricks. This room mirrors whoever sits longest. I’m just your reflection with a different ticket number. Don’t stare too hard; you’ll see yourself.

encounters.question: Are you waiting for someone to recognize you, or just for your number?

responses.text: Recognition’s the real ticket out. Numbers are just noise; a familiar glance could cut through years of queue. Keep looking and maybe I’m your missed connection.
Numbers are all that matter. Recognition’s a distraction, a glitch in the waiting. I don’t need your eyes; I need the speaker to say I’m next.

encounters.question: If they call my number before yours, will you still be here when I look back?

responses.text: I’ll linger, a smudge on the edge of your peripheral and here until you remember why my face itches at your memory. Go ahead, answer the call; I’ve got nowhere else.
I’ll vanish the second you turn. This place doesn’t keep leftovers once your turn’s up. Don’t look back; you’ll just see an empty chair.
